inline_review:
commit c6b895d141f61946a5f1aad31362625ec6d637e9
Author: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
mm/shmem: use invalidate_lock to fix hole-punch race

This commit introduces invalidate_lock to shmem_fault() and
a new shmem_map_pages() fault-around handler. The goal is to serialize
page fault PTE installation against fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) to prevent
use-after-free and bad page cache bugs.

> diff --git a/mm/shmem.c b/mm/shmem.c
> index 4ecefe02881d..5c654b86f3cf 100644
> --- a/mm/shmem.c
> +++ b/mm/shmem.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -2747,8 +2748,15 @@ static vm_fault_t shmem_fault(struct vm_fault *vmf)
>  	}
>  
>  	WARN_ON_ONCE(vmf->page != NULL);
> +	/*
> +	 * shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) holds invalidate_lock exclusive across
> +	 * unmap+truncate.  Take it shared here so shmem_fault cannot obtain
> +	 * a folio in the process of being punched.
> +	 */
> +	filemap_invalidate_lock_shared(mapping);
>  	err = shmem_get_folio_gfp(inode, vmf->pgoff, 0, &folio, SGP_CACHE,
>  				  gfp, vmf, &ret);
> +	filemap_invalidate_unlock_shared(mapping);
>  	if (err)
>  		return vmf_error(err);
>  	if (folio) {

Does dropping the lock here leave the TOCTOU race partially open?

The actual PTE installation happens later in the core MM code inside
finish_fault(). Because invalidate_lock_shared is dropped early,
shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) can acquire the exclusive lock right after
shmem_fault() returns, but before finish_fault() runs.

If this happens, shmem_fallocate() calls unmap_mapping_range(). Since the
PTE is not yet installed (pte_none is true), it does nothing. It then
proceeds to shmem_undo_range() and blocks waiting for the folio lock,
which is currently held by the fault path.

The fault path continues into finish_fault(), installs the PTE, increments
the mapcount, and unlocks the folio. This wakes up shmem_fallocate(),
which sees the folio is mapped and calls unmap_mapping_folio().

As noted in the commit message for Race 1, unmap_mapping_folio() is
vulnerable to a TOCTOU race with fork(). If this sequence occurs, doesn't
it recreate the exact BUG_ON(folio_mapped(folio)) this patch was
attempting to fix?

[ ... ]

> @@ -5268,9 +5278,26 @@ static const struct super_operations shmem_ops = {
>  #endif
>  };
>  
> +/*
> + * shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) holds invalidate_lock for write across
> + * unmap+truncate.  Take it for read here so fault-around cannot re-map
> + * pages being punched.
> + */
> +static vm_fault_t shmem_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
> +				  pgoff_t start_pgoff, pgoff_t end_pgoff)
> +{
> +	struct address_space *mapping = vmf->vma->vm_file->f_mapping;
> +	vm_fault_t ret;
> +
> +	filemap_invalidate_lock_shared(mapping);
> +	ret = filemap_map_pages(vmf, start_pgoff, end_pgoff);
> +	filemap_invalidate_unlock_shared(mapping);
> +	return ret;
> +}

Can this unconditional lock acquisition cause a priority inversion or hang?

do_fault_around() calls shmem_map_pages() while the faulting thread holds
mmap_lock for read. If shmem_fallocate() is concurrently holding the
exclusive invalidate_lock, the faulting thread will sleep here while still
holding mmap_lock.

This seems to bypass the explicit shmem_falloc_wait() mechanism inside
shmem_fault(), which drops mmap_lock before waiting to prevent system
hangs.

Sleeping with mmap_lock held for read allows any subsequent write-acquirer
to queue up, which would block all other threads in the process from
resolving page faults for the duration of the hole-punch.

Would it be safer to use filemap_invalidate_trylock_shared() here and
gracefully fall back to regular faulting upon failure?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Incomplete Fix / Race Condition: The patch fails to close the shmem_fault TOCTOU race (Race 2) because `invalidate_lock_shared` is dropped before the PTE is instantiated.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"1. `shmem_fault()` acquires `invalidate_lock_shared`, finds and locks the folio, and then drops `invalidate_lock_shared` before returning `VM_FAULT_LOCKED`.
2. The actual PTE installation occurs later in the core MM code inside `finish_fault()`.
3. Because the lock is dropped early, `shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE)` can acquire the exclusive `invalidate_lock` right after `shmem_fault()` returns, but before `finish_fault()` runs.
4. `shmem_fallocate()` calls `unmap_mapping_range()`. Since the PTE is not yet installed (`pte_none`), it does nothing.
5. `shmem_fallocate()` then proceeds to `shmem_truncate_range()` -> `shmem_undo_range()` and blocks on `folio_lock(folio)` (which is held by the fault path).
6. The fault path continues into `finish_fault()`, which checks `pte_none` (which is still true), installs the PTE, increments the mapcount, and unlocks the folio.
7. `shmem_fallocate` wakes up, sees the folio is mapped, and calls `unmap_mapping_folio()`.
8. As noted in the commit message for Race 1, `unmap_mapping_folio()` is notoriously vulnerable to a TOCTOU race with `fork()`. If this occurs, the child VMA retains the PTE, and `filemap_remove_folio()` hits the exact same `BUG_ON(folio_mapped(folio))` the patch attempted to fix.

The author's comparison to `page_mkwrite()` is structurally flawed: in `page_mkwrite()`, the PTE is already installed, so `unmap_mapping_range()` successfully clears it, causing the fault path to safely abort upon its subsequent `pte_same()` check. For a missing page fault, `unmap_mapping_range()` sees `pte_none` and does nothing, allowing `finish_fault()` to install the orphaned PTE."},{"problem":"Priority Inversion / Deadlock: Unconditionally blocking on `invalidate_lock_shared` in `shmem_map_pages()` defeats the `mmap_lock` dropping mechanism in `shmem_falloc_wait()`.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. `do_fault_around()` calls the new `shmem_map_pages()` while the faulting thread holds `mmap_lock` for read.
2. The patch makes `shmem_map_pages()` unconditionally acquire `filemap_invalidate_lock_shared()`. If `shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE)` is currently holding the exclusive `invalidate_lock`, the faulting thread will sleep while holding `mmap_lock`.
3. This bypasses the explicit `shmem_falloc_wait()` mechanism inside `shmem_fault()`, which was designed specifically to drop `mmap_lock` before waiting for a hole-punch to finish (a mechanism added to prevent a known system hang discovered by Trinity).
4. Sleeping with `mmap_lock` held for read allows any subsequent write-acquirer (e.g., `mprotect`, `munmap`) to queue up, which instantly starves all other threads in the process from resolving page faults. This freezes the entire process for the duration of the hole-punch.
5. Using `filemap_invalidate_trylock_shared()` and gracefully falling back to regular faulting upon failure is required to avoid this priority inversion."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit c6b895d141f61946a5f1aad31362625ec6d637e9
Author: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
mm/shmem: use invalidate_lock to fix hole-punch race

page fault PTE installation against fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) to prevent
use-after-free and bad page cache bugs.

> diff --git a/mm/shmem.c b/mm/shmem.c
> index 4ecefe02881d..5c654b86f3cf 100644
> --- a/mm/shmem.c
> +++ b/mm/shmem.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -2747,8 +2748,15 @@ static vm_fault_t shmem_fault(struct vm_fault *vmf)
>  	}
>  
>  	WARN_ON_ONCE(vmf->page != NULL);
> +	/*
> +	 * shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) holds invalidate_lock exclusive across
> +	 * unmap+truncate.  Take it shared here so shmem_fault cannot obtain
> +	 * a folio in the process of being punched.
> +	 */
> +	filemap_invalidate_lock_shared(mapping);
>  	err = shmem_get_folio_gfp(inode, vmf->pgoff, 0, &folio, SGP_CACHE,
>  				  gfp, vmf, &ret);
> +	filemap_invalidate_unlock_shared(mapping);
>  	if (err)
>  		return vmf_error(err);
>  	if (folio) {

The actual PTE installation happens later in the core MM code inside

If this happens, shmem_fallocate() calls unmap_mapping_range(). Since the

The fault path continues into finish_fault(), installs the PTE, increments

As noted in the commit message for Race 1, unmap_mapping_folio() is
vulnerable to a TOCTOU race with fork(). If this sequence occurs, doesn't

attempting to fix?

[ ... ]

> @@ -5268,9 +5278,26 @@ static const struct super_operations shmem_ops = {
>  #endif
>  };
>  
> +/*
> + * shmem_fallocate(PUNCH_HOLE) holds invalidate_lock for write across
> + * unmap+truncate.  Take it for read here so fault-around cannot re-map
> + * pages being punched.
> + */
> +static vm_fault_t shmem_map_pages(struct vm_fault *vmf,
> +				  pgoff_t start_pgoff, pgoff_t end_pgoff)
> +{
> +	struct address_space *mapping = vmf->vma->vm_file->f_mapping;
> +	vm_fault_t ret;
> +
> +	filemap_invalidate_lock_shared(mapping);
> +	ret = filemap_map_pages(vmf, start_pgoff, end_pgoff);
> +	filemap_invalidate_unlock_shared(mapping);
> +	return ret;
> +}

do_fault_around() calls shmem_map_pages() while the faulting thread holds
mmap_lock for read. If shmem_fallocate() is concurrently holding the

This seems to bypass the explicit shmem_falloc_wait() mechanism inside

Sleeping with mmap_lock held for read allows any subsequent write-acquirer

resolving page faults for the duration of the hole-punch.

Would it be safer to use filemap_invalidate_trylock_shared() here and
gracefully fall back to regular faulting upon failure?

"}
